content: I was exhausted and drugged with lack of sleep.
I told him what Snap and I had learned: the rays from the Moon, proving that Grantline had concentrated a considerable ore body.
"Does he know anything about this Grantline affair?"
Go to bed, Gregg, you need rest."
It was located aft, on the stern deck, near the stern watch tower.
We were well on our course to Mars.
The Moon had dwindled to a pin point of light beside the crescent Earth.
We sat at the ends, with the passengers on each of the sides.
And as pleasant as he is good looking.
His pointed face, accentuated by the pointed beard, was pallid.
"I am happy to meet you, sir." His voice was soft and deep.
A smile plucked at his thin, gray lips.
And as a matter of fact-"
"This is Sir Arthur Coniston, an English gentleman, lecturer and sky trotter-that is, he will be a sky trotter; he tells us he plans a number of voyages."
Snap introduced him as Rance Rankin.
An American-a quiet, blond fellow of thirty five or forty.
I ordered my breakfast and let the argument go on.
"I mean to say, I think I said too much.
mr Rankin, you are more diplomatic."
There were still three seats vacant at our table; I wondered who would occupy them.
I soon learned the answer-for one seat at least.
"She's on the deck, having orange juice.
At the Captain's table I saw the objects of my search: George Prince and his sister, one on each side of the Captain.
She was dressed in Earth fashion-white blouse and dark jacket, wide, knee length trousers of gray, with a red sash her only touch of color.
She went past me, flashed me a smile.
I answered her greeting, and met George Prince's casual gaze.
They were brother and sister, these Martians.
Gregg, I'm getting like you-too fanciful.
Do you know, from Venus to Earth, and I have no doubt on all of Mars, no man will please me more."
Of course, a chance to make a voyage with you-"
At our table-a big, good looking blond American.
Nice sort of fellow." She paused, then added, "If he's a professional entertainer, I'm a motor oiler."
"Why do you look so furtive?" she retorted.
By Satan, what a flow of words that devil driver can muster!
Venza, that prowler went through the smoking room!"
The purser looked up, followed the sound with his gaze.
What's going on, that's what I want to know?"
They wanted to know what kind of a ship this was.
They came to the corner of the deck, but turned back.
The empty deck chairs stood about.
The scream was stilled, but now we heard a commotion inside-the rasp of opening cabin doors; questions from frightened passengers.
I shouted, "Go back to your rooms!
dr Frank was in advance of Snap and me now.
He had a large heat ray projector in his hand.
Snap and I shoved back three or four passengers.
Snap, help me keep the crowd away." He shoved me forcibly.
From within, Carter was shouting, "Keep them out!
dr Frank went back into the room and banged the cabin door upon Snap and me.
Weapon in hand, Snap forced the panic stricken passengers back to their rooms.
I gazed around for her brother Miko, but did not see him.
Snap was threatening everybody with his cylinder.
"In there." I pounded on a twenty two.
I could see only Carter, but I heard the murmuring voice of dr Frank through the interior connecting door to a twenty.
Come in." He admitted the older officer and slammed the door upon me again. And immediately reopened it.
Tell them everything's all right. Miss Prince got frightened-that's all.
dr Frank is trying ... don't stand there like an ass, man.
I don't mean that! Tell Snap to watch his radio room.
Arm yourselves and guard our weapons."
I stammered, "If ... if she dies ... will you flash us word?"
He slammed the door upon me.
The turmoil of the ship gradually quieted.
Carter came into the chart room.
dr Frank and her brother are with her.
They're doing all they can." He told us what had happened.
The assailant opened it somehow, and assaulted Miss Prince-shot her in the chest with a heat ray.
But she did not see who did it.
I entered cautiously, switched on the dimmer of the tube lights, and searched the room.
It had only a bunk, my tiny desk, a chair and clothes robe.
Carter cut in on us from the chart room.
Someone was tampering with my door!
But it had done its work-awakened me.
I searched for it, pried it loose softly.
In the darkness, cylinder in hand, I crept softly from the bunk.
Upon impulse I reached for the door, jerked it open.
His electronized metallic robe burned my hands.
I lunged against him-I was almost as surprised as he.
The shock of my encounter, short circuited his robe; he materialized in the starlight.
He had dropped his hydrogen torch, and tried to grip me.
I fell helpless to the deck.
A white actinic light shot from it-caught us, bathed us. Snap had been awake; had heard the commotion of our encounter.
I'll shoot!" His warning siren rang out to alert the ship.
Miko ran with me a few steps.
Then he cursed and dropped me; fled away.
I fell like a sack of carbide to the deck.
I was in the chart room with Captain Carter, Snap and dr Frank bending over me.
I sat up, with dr Frank vigorously rubbing me.
"I'm all right." I told them what had happened.
I heard myself stammering, "Why-why we must get him!" I gathered my wits; a surge of hate swept me; a wild desire for vengeance.
Why don't you go get him?
"I'll bring him in here to you!
But I'll kill him, I tell you!"
We don't want him killed, not attacked, even.
